description:
We entered this canyon and made an attempt to explore it -- we were hoping there was a slight chance that we might be able to find the Mesa Shoulderband, a native land snail species which was observed here in 1953.

The lower part of the canyon was completely dominated by invasive plant species; it did however have a path or trail.

As we worked our way up the canyon, the trail petered out, and we had to use the narrow, mostly dry stream bed as a pathway. The stream bed consisted of large stones with a few small boulders. There were a few areas about halfway up the canyon stream bed which had retained water and supported aquatic life.

The upper half or two thirds of the canyon was virtually entirely filled with thick shrubs, was steep, and was difficult to enter.

This meant that we could not do much exploring in the part of the canyon which was mostly occupied still by native plants.
place_guess: La Jolla, San Diego, CA, US

The White Horehound was found at the Rice Canyon Open Space Preserve, however the plant is Native to Europe and invasive to California. The plant is an angiosperm, a dicot species. According to the American Botanical Council, the white horehound herb consist of ethnobotanical properties that is used as medicine for cough, both acute and chronic bronchitis, and throat aid.

Sweet fennel can be identified by the tall striated branching stems. It gives off the smell of anise or licorice. Leaves are alternately arranged, ovate and pinnately venated. The status of this plant is abundant in California. Sweet fennel can be an invasive species. It can serve as a food source for some grazing animals. It is used medicinally as a digestive aid as it aids in digestion and relieves muscle spasms.

Common Name: California brittlebush
Kumeyaay Name: Nahekwi

description:
My favorite Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=58029&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1

Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata) is a native, annual plant in the Lettuce, Chicory, Dandelion and Salsify (Cichorieae) tribe and the Asteraceae family. It grows in Shadscale Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, and Joshua Tree Woodland plant communities. The name "glabrata" refers to the leaves being (nearly) hairless. Ligules are pale yellow with red center growth ring. Outer phyllaries are glabrous or short-white hairy. Peak bloom time: March-June.

Jepson eFlora (includes botanical illustration and video): https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=4070

Calflora (includes species distribution map in CA): https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=5330

Anza-Borrego Desert Wildflowers: (excellent photos): https://borregowildflowers.org/?type=search&searchtype=S&family=&name=Malacothrix%20glabrata

Cichorieae is a distinct tribe within the Asteraceae family that is defined by its ligulate florets, meaning every floret has a strap‑shaped ligule rather than a differentiated petal limb. These ligules are five‑toothed at the tip and occur in heads composed entirely of ray‑like florets with no true disk florets present. Cichorieae are commonly herbs with milky latex, a trait pervasive across the tribe and visible in broken stems or leaves. Geographically, Cichorieae is most diverse in temperate Eurasia but extends worldwide, especially in open, disturbed, or seasonally dry habitats. Fruits are typically beaked achenes with a pappus of bristles or scales that aids in seed dispersal. The combination of milky sap, exclusively ligulate florets, and heads lacking any tubular disk florets defines this plant tribe.

Sunflowers, Daisies, Asters, and Allies (Asteraceae) family: Asteraceae is a huge family that "includes over 32,000 currently accepted species, in over 1,900 genera in 13 subfamilies, worldwide. The number of species in the family Asteraceae is rivaled only by Orchids (Orchidaceae): https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/47604-Asteraceae

Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris var. basilaris), a.k.a. Nopal in Spanish, is succulent plant in the Prickly Pears (Opuntia) genus and the Cacti (Cactaceae) family. Beavertail Pricklypear (Opuntia basilaris) is the species name. There are several subspecies, including the most common one, Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris var. basilaris), that grows in the arid regions of the Mojave Desert.
Indigenous people used Opuntia basilaris as food and carefully prepared the prickly pads and fruits.
8 traditional uses described here: http://naeb.brit.org/uses/search/?string=Opuntia+basilaris

Native American Ethnobotany: Traditional Native Plant Uses (medicines, foods, fibers, tools): http://naeb.brit.org/uses/search/?string=Opuntia+basilaris

Calflora: Opuntia basilaris: https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=5926

Beavertail Pricklypear (Opuntia basilaris) grows in many plant communties: Coastal Sage Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, Southern Oak Woodland, Chaparral, Valley Grassland, Pinyon-Juniper Woodland, and Joshua Tree Woodland. Pads are blue-green, 8--21 cm long by 5--13 cm wide, flat, and +- obovate. They are generally spineless, but have plenty of glochids--hairlike spines that remain in fingertips for weeks. Beautiful pink flowers attract many species of pollinators. Peak bloom time: April-June.

OpuntiaWeb: https://www.opuntiads.com/opuntia-basilaris-2/
"What is Opuntia basilaris? Opuntia basilaris is a distinctive prickly pear cactus; it has the archetypical “beaver tail” look. Mature plants form dense clumps 15-30 cm tall and 30-100 cm across. The pads are blue-green or grayish-green and are often wedge-shaped or broadly obovate. Pads are (7)15-17(22) cm long. Often the cladodes of this prickly pear are fan-shaped, but they may be oval, obovate, or even elongate-obovate. The pads of the major variety of this Opuntia (var basilaris) arise from a single point and form an approximate rosette. The areoles are slightly sunken. This prickly pear has blue-green or gray-green pads that are distinctive and that may have hints of purple in winter or drought, or they may be entirely purple under stress. Though spineless, there are numerous small glochids to be avoided. Flowers are magenta and about 7-8 cm across, very showy. Fruit is dry. The style is white or pinkish-white, and the stigma is white. Filaments are red, anthers are yellow. The seeds are large and thick (subspheric?) and angular. They measure 6.5-9 × 6.5-7 mm."

OpuntiaWeb: https://www.opuntiads.com/opuntia-basilaris-2/

Key Characteristics of Cacti (Cactaceae) family:
Cactaceae are mostly New World succulent perennials with specialized stem adaptations and unique reproductive traits. Members of the Cactaceae family are predominantly xerophytic plants adapted to arid environments, characterized by fleshy, photosynthetic stems that often replace leaves in function. These stems may be columnar, globular, or flattened into phylloclades. A defining feature is the presence of areoles—small cushion-like structures from which spines, glochids, flowers, and branches emerge. Leaves are typically absent or reduced to spines, except in basal genera like Pereskia, which retain functional foliage. Flowers are usually large, solitary, and epigynous, with numerous tepals transitioning from sepals to petals, and a profusion of stamens. The ovary is inferior and unilocular with parietal placentation, producing many-seeded berries. Root systems are shallow and fibrous, optimized for rapid moisture uptake. Pollination is primarily entomophilous, and the family is almost entirely native to the Americas, with Rhipsalis as a notable Old World exception.

Wild Canterbury Bells (Phacelia minor) is a native, annual, hairy-glandular plant in Borage Family (Boraginaceae) family that grows at higher elevations in the desert and in coastal sage scrub near the coast. Leaves are ovate to ± round with irregularly toothed margins. Corolla is purple, bellshaped with a slight constriction where base of petals meet throat. Anthers are white and exserted. Color is a paler purple compared to Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) that has dark, deep indigo blue flowers.

Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia), a.k.a. Desertbells, Desert Bluebells, California-bluebell, Desert Scorpionweed, and Desert Canterbury Bells, is a native, annual short-glandular-hairy plant in the Borage (Boraginaceae) family.
It is native to the Mojave and Sonoran deserts. It grows 10--70 cm (up to 28 inches) tall in sandy, gravelly desert soils. Stems are erect and branch from the base. Leaves are ovate to ± round with irregularly toothed margins. Corolla is bright indigo blue-purple and funnel-shaped with NO constriction at the throat (unlike Wild Canterbury Bells, Phacelia minor, that does have a slight constriction at the throat). Corolla has a diffuse or distinct white spots at base of sinuses. Anthers are long, white and exserted. Peak bloom time: February-April. There are 2 subspecies.

Phacelia campanularia var. vasiformis:
Conservation Status: Vulnerable (G3?) (NatureServe). It is
Endemic to southern California in the Mojave and Sonoran deserts at elevations less than 1600 m.

Borage (Boraginaceae) is a large family that includes: Baby Blue Eyes, Phacelias (Scorpionweeds), Eucrypta, Waterleaves, Forget-Me-Nots, and more. In general, most members of this family have bristly-hairy, prickly leaves and stems. They have alternately arranged leaves, or a combination of alternate and opposite leaves. The leaf blades usually have a narrow shape; many are linear or lance-shaped. They are smooth-edged or toothed, and some have petioles. Many have inflorescences that have a coiling shape, at least when new, called scorpioid cymes. The flower usually has a five-lobed calyx (5 petals). The corolla varies in shape and color from rotate to bell-shaped to tubular, but it generally has 5 lobes that can be green, white, yellow, orange, pink, purple, or blue. There are 5 stamens and one style with one or two stigmas. The fruit is called a drupe.

"Monkeyflowers used to be treated as Mimulus, but recent scientific literature has divided them between two genera, Diplacus and Erythranthe. Diplacus (which include the shrubby monkeyflowers) are characterized by flowers which are either sessile, or have pedicels shorter than the calyx, and which are persistent (remain attached) after flowering. Erythranthe have flowers with a pedicel longer than the calyx, and which are deciduous (fall) after flowering. Both Diplacus and Erythranthe include plants with comparatively large yellow or orange flowers, and plants with comparatively small magenta to purple flowers."

Lopseed (Phrymaceae) family is a small group of flowering plants found almost worldwide, but especially common in western North America and Australia. These plants grow in many different environments, including deserts, riverbanks, mountains, and even in shallow water. Most species are herbs, though a few grow as small shrubs. They can be annual or perennial, and some of the smallest flowering plants in the world belong to this family.
Plants in this family usually have simple, opposite leaves that may have toothed edges. Their flowers are an important identifying feature: they are tubular, bilaterally symmetrical, and have five petals fused at the base. The upper petal forms a short “lip,” while the lower petal is often larger and divided into three lobes. Inside the flower, there are four stamens arranged in two pairs, and a long style ending in a two‑lobed stigma that closes when it touches pollen. The fruits are usually dry capsules filled with many seeds, though a few species produce different fruit types.
What makes the Lopseed family different from many other plant families is its wide variation in flower structure and reproduction. Some species rely on insects for pollination, others on hummingbirds, and some can self‑fertilize or reproduce asexually. This level of variation makes the family harder to define by appearance alone, and its classification has changed several times as scientists have studied its genetics. In fact, the family used to contain only one species until molecular research showed that many plants once placed in other families actually belonged here.

Freckled Milkvetch (Astragalus lentiginosus) is a native, annual or perennial plant in the Milkvetches, Locoweeds, and Allies (Galegeae) tribe and the Legumes (Fabaceae) family. Peak bloom time: May-June. Calflora list 19 subspecies, so there is great variation to how it looks. It is found in "Sagebrush Scrub, Shadscale Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, Alkali Sink, Yellow Pine Forest, Subalpine Forest, Foothill Woodland, Valley Grassland, and Joshua Tree Woodland plant communities."

Milkvetches, Locoweeds, and Allies (Galegeae) tribe are plants in the Legumes (Fabaceae) family that are characterized by herbaceous or shrubby growth, compound leaves with well‑defined stipules, and papilionoid flowers with a standard, wings, and keel. Its members typically produce elongated, dehiscent legumes with a visible replum and seeds arranged in a single series. Many species accumulate non‑protein amino acids, a chemical trait associated with the broader hologalegina lineage. Vegetative morphology often includes pinnate leaves with numerous leaflets and a lack of tendrils. Floral structure is usually zygomorphic with a persistent calyx and a keel enclosing the reproductive organs.

Legumes (Fabaceae) family, a.k.a. Pea family, is a very large family of flowering plants. Traditionally the Pea Family was divided into several subfamilies, including:

1 Subfamily Faboideae. Familiar western genera include: Astragalus, Dalea, LUPINUS, and Trifolium.
2 Acacias, Mimosas, Mesquites, and Allies (Subfamily Mimosoideae)
3 Peacock Flower Subfamily (Subfamily Caesalpinioideae), includes Palo Verde (Genus Parkinsonia).

Fabaceae is a vast plant family defined by its ecological and economic importance, encompassing trees, shrubs, vines, and herbs that thrive across nearly all climates. Its members are notable for their ability to fix atmospheric nitrogen through symbiosis with root-dwelling bacteria, a trait that enriches soils and underpins agricultural systems worldwide. The family includes staple food crops such as beans, peas, lentils, and soy, as well as forage plants like alfalfa and clover, making it central to both human diets and livestock feed. Beyond food, Fabaceae provides timber, gums, dyes, and medicinal compounds, while also shaping ecosystems by stabilizing soils and supporting diverse wildlife. Its adaptability and utility have made it one of the most influential plant families in human history and in natural landscapes alike.

Legumes (Fabaceae) family, a.k.a. Pea family, is a very large family of flowering plants. It includes trees, shrubs, and perennial or annual herbaceous plants, which are easily recognized by their fruit (legume) and their compound, stipulate leaves. Fabaceae is the most common family of flowering plants found in tropical rainforests and dry forests of the Americas and Africa. The family is widely distributed, and is the third-largest land plant family in number of species, only behind the Orchidaceae and Asteraceae, with about 765 genera and nearly 20,000 known species, worldwide. Traditionally the Pea Family was divided into three families or subfamilies: Mimosoidea (Mimosa Family), Caesalpinioidea (Senna Family), and the Papilionoidea (Fabaceae)": https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/47122-Fabaceae

description:
Fountain Grass (Cenchrus setaceus) is an introduced/naturalized, perennial, non-native, highly invasive bunch grass in the Grasses (Poaceae) family. A.k.a. Crimson Fountaingrass. Unfortunately, this attractive grass is sold in many plant nurseries as an ornamental and is still being planted in many gardens because of its high tolerance to wind and drought. It is drought-tolerant, grows fast, reaches 3 feet in height, and has many purple, plumose flower spikes. Airborne seeds spread easily and displace native grasses.

Only INaturalist calls Fountain Grass by this scientific name: Cenchrus setaceus (as of 12/22/23).

Wild Canterbury Bells (Phacelia minor) is a native, annual, hairy-glandular plant in Borage Family (Boraginaceae) family that grows at higher elevations in the desert and in coastal sage scrub near the coast. Leaves are ovate to ± round with irregularly toothed margins. Corolla is purple, bellshaped with a slight constriction where base of petals meet throat. Anthers are white and exserted. Color is a paler purple compared to Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) that has dark, deep indigo blue flowers.

Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia), a.k.a. Desertbells, Desert Bluebells, California-bluebell, Desert Scorpionweed, and Desert Canterbury Bells, is a native, annual short-glandular-hairy plant in the Borage (Boraginaceae) family.
It is native to the Mojave and Sonoran deserts. It grows 10--70 cm (up to 28 inches) tall in sandy, gravelly desert soils. Stems are erect and branch from the base. Leaves are ovate to ± round with irregularly toothed margins. Corolla is bright indigo blue-purple and funnel-shaped with NO constriction at the throat (unlike Wild Canterbury Bells, Phacelia minor, that does have a slight constriction at the throat). Corolla has a diffuse or distinct white spots at base of sinuses. Anthers are long, white and exserted. Peak bloom time: February-April. There are 2 subspecies.

Phacelia campanularia var. vasiformis:
Conservation Status: Vulnerable (G3?) (NatureServe). It is
Endemic to southern California in the Mojave and Sonoran deserts at elevations less than 1600 m.

Borage (Boraginaceae) is a large family that includes: Baby Blue Eyes, Phacelias (Scorpionweeds), Eucrypta, Waterleaves, Forget-Me-Nots, and more. In general, most members of this family have bristly-hairy, prickly leaves and stems. They have alternately arranged leaves, or a combination of alternate and opposite leaves. The leaf blades usually have a narrow shape; many are linear or lance-shaped. They are smooth-edged or toothed, and some have petioles. Many have inflorescences that have a coiling shape, at least when new, called scorpioid cymes. The flower usually has a five-lobed calyx (5 petals). The corolla varies in shape and color from rotate to bell-shaped to tubular, but it generally has 5 lobes that can be green, white, yellow, orange, pink, purple, or blue. There are 5 stamens and one style with one or two stigmas. The fruit is called a drupe.
(Some Borage examples found in and near Anza Borrego State Park, CA: Anza-Borrego Desert Wildflowers: https://borregowildflowers.org/?type=search&searchtype=C&family=Borage&name=)

My favorite flowering Desert Needles (Palafoxia arida var. arida) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?taxon_id=78333&user_id=aparrot1

Spanish Needle (Palafoxia arida) and ssp. Desert Needles (Palafoxia arida var. arida) is a native, annual, short-hairy plant in the and Bahieae Tribe, Asteroideae subfamily, and the large Asteraceae family. It grows up to 60cm (up to 24 inches) tall on sandy flats and in desert washes. Stem is erect, many-branching, and glandular. Leaves are gray-green, simple, rough, hairy, linear to lance-linear, and 2-12 cm long. Phyllaries are linear, scabrous (scaly), glandular and hairy. Inflorescence is a long, narrow, cylindrical head with disk flowers only. Disk flowers are white. Anthers are deep pink to purple. Peak bloom time: February-May. Indigenous people (like the local Cahuilla of the Coachella Valley) used this plant to produce a yellow dye for basketry.

My favorite California Primrose (Eulobus californicus) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=77107&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1

California Primrose (Eulobus californicus), a.k.a. California Suncup, and formerly called Camissonia californica, is a native, annual plant in the Evening-Primrose (Onagraceae) family. Leaves are irregularly and sharply pinnately lobed and narrowly elliptic. Flowers have 8 stamens and 4 yellow petals that are finely flecked with red dots near the base. Peak bloom time: February-May. Fruits are narrow, 45--110 mm long, reflexed and cylindrical. It is found in Arizona, California, and Baja California, Mexico and grows in Coastal Sage Scrub, Foothill Woodland, Chaparral, and Valley Grassland plant communities.

Calflora (includes species distribution map in CA): https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=11681

Evening-primrose (Onagraceae) family:
Taxon Page: Jepson eFlora: https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=24343
"Genera In Family: 22 genera, +- 657 species: worldwide, especially western North America. Leaf: cauline or basal, alternate, opposite, or whorled, generally simple and toothed. . ."

Evening-primrose (Onagraceae) family: 18 genera and 655 species concentrated in the temperate region of the New World. The family is characterized by flowers with parts mostly on the plan of four: four sepals, four petals, four or eight stamens, but there are some exceptions. The ovary is inferior (i.e., below the flower proper). In the temperate zone, the family is known for genera such as Epilobium, including California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum) and Fireweed (Epilobium angustifolium). Another well-known genus is Oenothera (with about 80 species), which has been of great importance in studies in genetics and evolution.

description:
Saharan Mustard (Brassica tournefortii) growing along the road. The plant is BAD news when it takes over vast areas of desert and crowds out the native plants (as it’s doing in the Coachella Valley Preserve nearby).

My favorite Saharan Mustard (Brassica tournefortii) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=75870&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1
Flowers: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/339358821

Saharan Mustard (Brassica tournefortii) is an annual, introduced/naturalized, highly INVASIVE plant in the Cabbages and Mustards (Brassica) genus, Mustard Family (Brassicaceae, a.k.a. Cruciferae), and the Mustards, Capers, and Allies (Brassicales) order that grows up to 80 cm (32 inches) tall. Peak bloom time for the pale yellow flowers: December-June.

"[Cal-IPC] Habitat, Ecological impact: Brassica tournefortii (Saharan mustard or African mustard) is a winter annual (family Brassicaceae) found in deserts, desert dunes, and coastal scrub, including the San Joaquin Valley, Sonoran and Mojave Deserts, and southwestern region of California. Saharan mustard readily invades newly burned areas, and is known to increase fire frequency and fuel load. Increased fire frequency can cause scrub habitats to convert to grasslands because the native shrubs are not adapted to recurrent fires. The high biomass of Saharan mustard, along with frequent fires, may deplete soils of important nutrients, making native habitat recovery more difficult. Cal-IPC Rating: High."

Cal-IPC: https://www.cal-ipc.org/plants/profile/brassica-tournefortii-profile/

Calflora (includes species distribution map in CA): https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=1146

Mustard Family (Brassicaceae):
Jepson eFlora: Taxon Description: https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=70
"Genera In Family: +- 330 genera, 3780 species: worldwide, especially temperate. Note: Highest diversity in Mediterranean area, mountains of southwestern Asia, adjacent central Asia, western North America; some Brassica species are oil or vegetable crops. . ."

Mustard Family (Brassicaceae, a.k.a. Cruciferae):
What sets this family apart from other plant families? It is a combination of 3 features that show up together only in Brassicaceae: the cross‑shaped four‑petal flower, the “four long + two short” stamens, and the pod‑type fruit with a replum holding the seeds. Their sulfur‑based chemicals are another strong clue and explain why many of these plants taste spicy or bitter. These traits make the family easy to recognize compared to most other flowering plants.
Brassicaceae plants are mostly small herbs with simple leaves and a sharp smell or taste because they make sulfur‑based chemicals. Their flowers almost always have four petals arranged like a cross. They also have a very steady stamen pattern: six stamens total, with four tall ones and two short ones. Their fruits are pods that split open from both sides. Inside the pod is a thin, stiff inner wall called the replum. The replum is the part that stays behind after the pod opens, and the seeds are attached to it. Many familiar vegetables—like cabbage, broccoli, radish, and mustard—belong to this family.

Recognizing Plant Families of the West: a Field Guide, published by U.S. Department of the Interior, Bureau of Land Management (BLM), 2011

Saharan Mustard (Brassica tournefortii) growing along the road. The plant is BAD news when it takes over vast areas of desert and crowds out the native plants (as it’s doing in the Coachella Valley Preserve nearby).

My favorite Saharan Mustard (Brassica tournefortii) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=75870&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1
Flowers: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/339358821

Saharan Mustard (Brassica tournefortii) is an annual, introduced/naturalized, highly INVASIVE plant in the Cabbages and Mustards (Brassica) genus, Mustard Family (Brassicaceae, a.k.a. Cruciferae), and the Mustards, Capers, and Allies (Brassicales) order that grows up to 80 cm (32 inches) tall. Peak bloom time for the pale yellow flowers: December-June.

"[Cal-IPC] Habitat, Ecological impact: Brassica tournefortii (Saharan mustard or African mustard) is a winter annual (family Brassicaceae) found in deserts, desert dunes, and coastal scrub, including the San Joaquin Valley, Sonoran and Mojave Deserts, and southwestern region of California. Saharan mustard readily invades newly burned areas, and is known to increase fire frequency and fuel load. Increased fire frequency can cause scrub habitats to convert to grasslands because the native shrubs are not adapted to recurrent fires. The high biomass of Saharan mustard, along with frequent fires, may deplete soils of important nutrients, making native habitat recovery more difficult. Cal-IPC Rating: High."

Cal-IPC: https://www.cal-ipc.org/plants/profile/brassica-tournefortii-profile/

Mustard Family (Brassicaceae, a.k.a. Cruciferae):
What sets this family apart from other plant families? It is a combination of 3 features that show up together only in Brassicaceae: the cross‑shaped four‑petal flower, the “four long + two short” stamens, and the pod‑type fruit with a replum holding the seeds. Their sulfur‑based chemicals are another strong clue and explain why many of these plants taste spicy or bitter. These traits make the family easy to recognize compared to most other flowering plants.
Brassicaceae plants are mostly small herbs with simple leaves and a sharp smell or taste because they make sulfur‑based chemicals. Their flowers almost always have four petals arranged like a cross. They also have a very steady stamen pattern: six stamens total, with four tall ones and two short ones. Their fruits are pods that split open from both sides. Inside the pod is a thin, stiff inner wall called the replum. The replum is the part that stays behind after the pod opens, and the seeds are attached to it. Many familiar vegetables—like cabbage, broccoli, radish, and mustard—belong to this family.

Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata) is a native, annual plant in the Lettuce, Chicory, Dandelion and Salsify (Cichorieae) tribe and the Asteraceae family. It grows in Shadscale Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, and Joshua Tree Woodland plant communities. The name "glabrata" refers to the leaves being (nearly) hairless. Ligules are pale yellow with red center growth ring. Outer phyllaries are glabrous or short-white hairy. Peak bloom time: March-June.

Cichorieae is a distinct tribe within the Asteraceae family that is defined by its ligulate florets, meaning every floret has a strap‑shaped ligule rather than a differentiated petal limb. These ligules are five‑toothed at the tip and occur in heads composed entirely of ray‑like florets with no true disk florets present. Cichorieae are commonly herbs with milky latex, a trait pervasive across the tribe and visible in broken stems or leaves. Geographically, Cichorieae is most diverse in temperate Eurasia but extends worldwide, especially in open, disturbed, or seasonally dry habitats. Fruits are typically beaked achenes with a pappus of bristles or scales that aids in seed dispersal. The combination of milky sap, exclusively ligulate florets, and heads lacking any tubular disk florets defines this plant tribe.

description: Today was busy at adobe falls. I didn’t have the key to the lower gate with me so I had to take the unconventional route under the 8 freeway. I passed two separate groups of people, the second group was surprisingly large - about ten guys playing football around the lower warerfall area that drains northward. I continued east towards the clay lens grasslands where I came upon the site of a large crew of workers clearing massive stands of vegetation. It was a delight to see the Castor bean and mustard getting cleared as well as some arundo down in the riparian area. Minding my business, I diverted off the trail towards the grasslands where I found a high diversity of liverworts. Doubling back towards the falls I finally saw the vast area of riparian woodland that had been cleared of its trees and shrubs. It was quite incredible and unbelievable to see the amount of vegetation that had been removed. It looked like a wasteland. However a closer look determined this to be a targeted removal of the invasive species like Washingtonia that once dominated this area. The stumps can be seen in the foreground of these photos, with other Washingtonias still intact in the background - I’m assuming these will be gone too in the following days.
place_guess: Del Cerro, San Diego, CA, US

Arrowweed (Pluchea sericea), a.k.a Arrow Weed, Marsh Fleabane, and Cachanilla in Spanish, is a native, perennial, desert shrub in the Asteraceae family that grows up to 3m (9ft) tall in Coastal Sage Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, and Wetland-Riparian plant communities. It tolerates many types of soil including alkaline soils. Leaf blades are lanceolate to narrowly lanceolate or narrowly oblanceolate, 1–5 × 0.2–1 cm long, margins entire, faces sparsely to densely silvery sericeous (covered with fine silky hair). Flower heads are crowded at the stem tips and contain many lavender-pink disk flowers surrounded by hairy, pinkish bracts. Peak bloom time: March-July. Indigenous people had many uses for this plant medicinally, as tools, and for building material. They used the long, straight stems to produce arrow shafts for hunting. 28 traditional uses are described here:

Chuparosa (Justicia californica), a.k.a. Hummingbird Flower and Rama Blanca in Spanish, is a native, perennial shrub in the Acanthus (Acanthaceae) family that grows up to 2-3 m (7-8 ft) tall in dry, sandy desert soils in Creosote Bush Scrub plant community. Leaves are opposite, petioled, triangular, or rounded, and puberulent. Stems are canescent (covered with short, white or gray hairs), and without grooves. Flowers are usually reddish-orange, but occasionally yellow. Flowers are narrowly tubular, 2-lipped, and have 2 stamens. Flowering and fruiting can occur throughout the year, depending on rainfall. Chuparosa is a favorite nectar plant for hummingbirds.

"Genera In Family: 220 genera, 4000 species: especially tropics; some ornamental: Justicia (Beloperone, shrimp-plant), Acanthus, Thunbergia. . ."

Acanthus (Acanthaceae) family is a large group of flowering plants found mainly in tropical and subtropical regions, with its highest diversity in Southeast Asia, Africa, Brazil, and Central America. A few species reach warm temperate areas, but most thrive in humid tropical forests, coastal zones, wetlands, and scrublands. Acanthus family plants are usually herbs or shrubs, though some are vines or small trees. They almost always have simple leaves arranged in opposite pairs, and many species contain cystoliths, which are tiny mineral deposits that appear as streaks or bumps on the leaves. Their flowers are typically tubular and bilaterally symmetrical, often held in spikes or clusters and usually backed by noticeable bracts. Inside the flower, there are usually two or four stamens, and the ovary has two chambers. The fruit is a dry capsule that opens forcefully, flinging the seeds away with the help of a small hooklike structure. This explosive seed release is one of the most distinctive traits of the family and helps separate it from many other plant groups.

description:
HIGHLY INVASIVE Fountain Grass growing in Palm Canyon in the Colorado Desert.

Fountain Grass (Cenchrus setaceus), a.k.a. Crimson Fountaingrass, is an introduced/naturalized, perennial, non-native, HIGHLY INVASIVE bunch grass in the Grasses (Poaceae) family. It is drought-tolerant, grows fast, reaches 3 feet in height, and has many pink to dark burgundy, plumose flower spikes. Spikelets are 2.5-7mm long. This fountaingrass is well adapted to fire, and plants can recover to pre-burn density, even increase in density, following a burn. It is cultivated as an ornamental. Unfortunately, this attractive grass is sold in many plant nurseries as an ornamental and is still being planted in many gardens because of its high tolerance to wind and drought. Airborne seeds spread easily and displace native grasses.

Only INaturalist calls Fountain Grass by this scientific name: Cenchrus setaceus (as of 12/15/24)

Irene's fruiting and flowering Fountain Grass observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?taxon_id=430581&user_id=aparrot1&verifiable=any

Poaceae, a.k.a. Gramineae, has 650--900 genera in family and +- 10550 species worldwide. "It has the greatest economic importance of any family (wheat, rice, maize, millet, sorghum, sugar cane, forage crops, ornamental, weeds; thatching, weaving, building materials).
Habit: Annual to woody perennial herb; roots generally fibrous. Stem: generally round, hollow; nodes swollen, solid. Leaf: alternate, 2-ranked, generally linear, parallel-veined; sheath generally open; ligule membranous or hairy, at blade base. Inflorescence: various (of generally many spikelets). Spikelet: glumes generally 2; florets (lemma, palea, flower) 1--many; lemma generally membranous, sometimes glume-like; palea generally +- transparent, +- enclosed by lemma. Flower: generally bisexual, minute; perianth vestigial; stamens generally 3; stigmas generally 2, generally plumose. Fruit: grain, sometimes achene- or utricle-like. Generally wind-pollinated."

How to Photograph Grasses for I.D: Take 3-4 photos showing:
Whole clump of grass
Flower (if present)
Ligule (the piece of tissue between the grass blade and the stem, the grass "armpit." Ligule is the membranous flap or series of hairs at the junction of the sheath and blade of the grass leaf.

Gander's Cholla (Cylindropuntia ganderi ssp. ganderi), a.k.a. Gander's Buckhorn Cholla, is a native, perennial cactus in the Chollas (Cylindropuntia) genus that grows in sandy, gravelly soil. It has multiple, almost linear ascending pale green stems. Spines are < 2.5 cm, white to pale yellow. Flowers have yellow tepals, green filaments, and yellow anthers. The flowers cluster around the stem tips. Peak bloom time: March-June. Fruits are tan, densely spiny or bur-like.
The spiny Cholla (genus Cylindropuntia) is a favorite nesting location for the large (22cm, 8.5 inch) desert bird, the Cactus Wren. Link to a Cactus Wren (Campylorhynchus brunneicapillus) observation: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/148634524 and a nest in Gander's Cholla: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/199168021

Key Characteristics of Cacti (Cactaceae) family:
Cactaceae are mostly New World succulent perennials with specialized stem adaptations and unique reproductive traits. Members of the Cactaceae family are predominantly xerophytic plants adapted to arid environments, characterized by fleshy, photosynthetic stems that often replace leaves in function. These stems may be columnar, globular, or flattened into phylloclades. A defining feature is the presence of areoles—small cushion-like structures from which spines, glochids, flowers, and branches emerge. Leaves are typically absent or reduced to spines, except in basal genera like Pereskia, which retain functional foliage. Flowers are usually large, solitary, and epigynous, with numerous tepals transitioning from sepals to petals, and a profusion of stamens. The ovary is inferior and unilocular with parietal placentation, producing many-seeded berries. Root systems are shallow and fibrous, optimized for rapid moisture uptake. Pollination is primarily entomophilous, and the family is almost entirely native to the Americas, with Rhipsalis as a notable Old World exception.

COMPARE 2 Wild Buckwheats (Eriogonum genus): both grow in the Mojave Desert:

xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

My favorite Desert Trumpet (Eriogonum inflatum) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=59459&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1

Inflated Stems: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/193847667
Vast Orange Carpet: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/326145813

Desert Trumpet (Eriogonum inflatum) is a native, perennial plant in the Wild Buckwheats (Eriogonum) genus that grows 1--10 dm (up to 39 inches) tall in Sagebrush Scrub, Creosote Bush Scrub, Pinyon-Juniper Woodland, and Joshua Tree Woodland plant communities. It is distinguished by stems that are (usually) inflated just below the branching segments. The plant only has basal leaves, +- round, and short-hairy. Yellow flowers are tiny, hairy, only 2-3 mm wide, 5 petals with pointed tips, with yellow exserted anthers. Fruits are 2--2.5 mm and glabrous. Desert Trumpet can bloom anytime of year given adequate moisture. Indigenous people had many uses for this plant. Cahuilla, the native people near Palm Springs, CA, brewed a strong, black decoction, made from the leaves of various species of buckwheat, to cure headaches and stomach disorders. The oldest plants are the most effective as medicine. 7 traditional uses are described here: http://naeb.brit.org/uses/search/?string=Eriogonum+inflatum

Little Desert Trumpet (Eriogonum trichopes) is a native, annual plant in the Knotweed (Polygonaceae) family that grows 1–4.5 dm (up to 17.5 inches) tall in sandy or gravelly soil in Creosote Bush Scrub and Joshua Tree Woodland plant communities. Peduncles (above the inflated stem) are mostly erect. Flowers are yellow and bloom throughout the year when there is adequate rainfall.
